item.title: Working With Rust Channels
item.description: Channels are an abstraction for sharing data between concurrently-running threads. For an example, you have a mapreduce system, and map and reduce threads are running parallelly. Each mapped output needs to be reduced using a reducer thread. Instead of passing data to a shared memory, which would require locks, you could just pass data to a channel and it would be received by the corresponding receiver thread. Using [[Go]]’s language documentation,
item.content:
Channels are an abstraction for sharing data between concurrently-running threads. For an example, you have a mapreduce system, and map and reduce threads are running parallelly. Each mapped output needs to be reduced using a reducer thread. Instead of passing data to a shared memory, which would require locks, you could just pass data to a channel and it would be received by the corresponding receiver thread. Using [[Go]]’s language documentation,

Do not communicate by sharing memory; instead, share memory by communicating.

Example: Implement a chat system or a system where many threads perform parts of a calculation and send the parts to one thread that aggregates the results. Channels are supplied by std::sync, and a type of channel called mpsc is used in the book, which stands for multiple producer single consumer.

use std::sync::mpsc;
use std::thread

fn main() {
  let (tx, rx) = mpsc::channel(); 
  thread::spawn(move || {
    let val = String::from("hi"); 
    tx.send(val).unwrap(); 
  }); 
  let received = rx.recv().unwrap(); 
  println!("Got: {}", received);
}

Code Synopsis:

  • thread::spawn used to spawn new thread
  • move used so that the new thread owns the tx object
  • tx.send needs to be unwrap()ped because receiving end might’ve been dropped, so we might have errors.
  • recv() call does not block, instead returns Result<T, E>, so that if no data is available, it returns an ‘Error’.
  • Example of using a channel’s non-blocking “recv” function- 
    enum Message{
  JoinRequest(String),
  DataRequest(String)
}

let (transmitter, receiver) = mpsc::channel();
thread::spawn(move || {
  let x = Message::DataRequest(String::from("I want data"));
  let y = Message::JoinRequest(String::from("I want to join!"));
  transmitter.send(x).unwrap();
  thread::sleep(time::Duration::from_secs(2));
  transmitter.send(y).unwrap();
});

for item in receiver{
  match item {
    Message::JoinRequest(join) => {
      println!("I got {}", join);
    },
    Message::DataRequest(data) => {
      println!("I got {}", data);
    }
  }
}
    • In the same way, data can be “tagged” while passing to a channel with enums. This is a simple example, which can be used in cases like, when we have peers in a network that listen on a single port for both “join” request of new nodes, and “sync” requests to request for data. However, using enums is not really a good idea, since Rust allocates for the largest size in receiver. Instead, we could use Box to point to different types of data that can be available. Or Arc if it needs to be passed to multiple threads.

A variable cannot be “reused” after being sent down the channel, because the receiving thread might drop it or change it. So a third println!("I sent {:?} down the channel", y); would fail in the above code.

item.title: Namespaces in Linux
item.description: Namespaces decide what subset of resources the processes running on a system can access or “see”. With namespaces, we can have isolated mountpoints, PID spaces (so that two processes on the OS can think their PID is the same), hostnames and network interfaces isolated to processes. These resources may have a different mapping on the host system. For an example, the first process running inside a PID namespace might think that its PID is 1 but in the host system, it is actually mapped to some other PID, like 1234.
item.content:
Namespaces decide what subset of resources the processes running on a system can access or “see”. With namespaces, we can have isolated mountpoints, PID spaces (so that two processes on the OS can think their PID is the same), hostnames and network interfaces isolated to processes. These resources may have a different mapping on the host system. For an example, the first process running inside a PID namespace might think that its PID is 1 but in the host system, it is actually mapped to some other PID, like 1234.

The list of running namespaces in the host system can be extracted by running the lsns command. Namespaces provide the basis of isolation between processes running on the operating system and allocateable resources. There are various types of namespaces in linux. Some of the namespaces being used to build BeeJudge1 are explained briefly below:

PID Namespaces

PID namespaces allow the isolation of process identifiers (PIDs) between different namespaces. As such, two processes running on the host system might think that they have the same PID when in reality they are different. Newly created namespaces assign PID of 1 to the first run process inside the namespace and subsequent PIDs to the processes after that. For a simple demonstration, let’s look at the output after creating a new PID namespace with bash:

Create a new PID namespace with the unshare command:

$ sudo unshare -p /bin/sh

On the host system:

$ ps -ax | grep /bin/sh
1018662 pts/2    S      0:00 sudo unshare -pf /bin/sh
1018663 pts/2    S      0:00 unshare -pf /bin/sh
1018664 pts/2    S+     0:00 /bin/sh

Inside the namespace:

# echo $$
1

Here we can see that although the sh process has PID of 1018664 on the host system, it has a PID of 1 inside the new namespace we created. We can also see that the PID namespaces are different by running the following commands on the system:

On the host system:

$ sudo ls -lah /proc/1018664/ns
total 0
dr-x--x--x 2 root root 0 Nov 16 01:55 .
dr-xr-xr-x 9 root root 0 Nov 16 01:50 ..
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:55 cgroup -> 'cgroup:[4026531835]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:55 ipc -> 'ipc:[4026531839]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:55 mnt -> 'mnt:[4026531840]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:55 net -> 'net:[4026531992]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:55 pid -> 'pid:[4026532764]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:55 pid_for_children -> 'pid:[4026532764]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:55 user -> 'user:[4026531837]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:55 uts -> 'uts:[4026531838]'

Inside the namespace:

# ls -lah /proc/$$/ns
total 0
dr-x--x--x 2 root root 0 Nov 16 01:39 .
dr-xr-xr-x 9 root root 0 Oct  6 05:02 ..
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:39 cgroup -> 'cgroup:[4026531835]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:39 ipc -> 'ipc:[4026531839]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:39 mnt -> 'mnt:[4026531840]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:39 net -> 'net:[4026531992]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:39 pid -> 'pid:[4026531836]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:53 pid_for_children -> 'pid:[4026531836]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:39 user -> 'user:[4026531837]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Nov 16 01:39 uts -> 'uts:[4026531838]'

We can clearly see the different values for the PID-related namespaces.

Now try running a ps -ef before proceeding to the next section. Although we can see all processes running on the host system, the processes cannot be killed from the new namespace. Try doing it 😄

Mount Namespaces

If you tried the above command in your system and ran a ps -ef command, you would be surprised to see that all the processes running on the host system are enumerated in the output. Technically it seems like there was no isolation of PIDs whatsoever! This happens due to a reason. When we created a new namespace, the old mountpoints are copied along to the new namespace. And commands like ps work on /proc mount to extract information about running processes.

In order to avoid this complication, we will depend on mount namespaces. First of all, we will create a new PID namespace along with a mount namespace and fork a /bin/sh inside it as usual.

Create the namespace on host machine

$ mkdir /tmp/namespaceroot
$ unshare -pmf /bin/sh

Why we created a namespaceroot folder will be evident shortly. Now inside the namespace, we run a series of commands to mount a temporary filesystem

Inside the namespace

# mount -t tmpfs tmpfs /tmp/namespaceroot
# mkdir -p /tmp/namespaceroot/proc
# mount -t proc proc /tmp/namespaceroot/proc
# ps -ef
UID          PID    PPID  C STIME TTY          TIME CMD
root           1       0  0 02:36 pts/3    00:00:00 /bin/sh
root          18       1  0 02:46 pts/3    00:00:00 bash
root          45      18  0 02:47 pts/3    00:00:00 bash
root          46      45  0 02:47 pts/3    00:00:00 tee -a /root/.bash_history
root          47      45  0 02:47 pts/3    00:00:00 logger -t root[18]
root          48      18  0 02:48 pts/3    00:00:00 ps -ef
# ls /proc
1          cpuinfo      ioports      mdstat        schedstat      timer_list
18         crypto       irq          meminfo       scsi           tty
49         devices      kallsyms     misc          self           uptime
50         diskstats    kcore        modules       slabinfo       version
51         dma          keys         mounts        softirqs       version_signature
52         driver       key-users    mpt           spl            vmallocinfo
acpi       execdomains  kmsg         mtrr          stat           vmstat
buddyinfo  fb           kpagecgroup  net           swaps          zoneinfo
bus        filesystems  kpagecount   pagetypeinfo  sys
cgroups    fs           kpageflags   partitions    sysrq-trigger
cmdline    interrupts   loadavg      pressure      sysvipc
consoles   iomem        locks        sched_debug   thread-self

and the findmount command returns a pretty picture as well. The output is too long to list here but you should have a good idea from it as well.

Along with proc, we will also isolate the /tmp mount

On the host

$ mkdir -p /tmp/newtmp

Inside the namespace

# mount -o bind,nodev /tmp/newtmp /tmp
# ls /tmp

Now if you create any file inside the /tmp/newtmp folder in host system, it should be readable inside the namespace.

  1. BeeJudge is a planned project, which has been suspended to lack of access to a linux VM/Machine. It is a code-judging platform for linux. ↩︎
